input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The study focuses on a Living Lab building used as a research platform in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain. The physical twin comprises the actual building infrastructure (envelope, HVAC, lighting and other MEP systems), a dense sensor network for temperature, humidity, occupancy and energy consumption, and actuators for controllable equipment. Its static information is captured in a Building Information Model (BIM). The Lab operates continuously, providing heterogeneous real‑time data streams that make it a suitable candidate for a Digital Twin deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of all sensor streams, interactive 3D visualization of the building state, simulation of “what‑if” scenarios, life‑cycle‑wide optimization of energy and comfort, predictive analytics for maintenance, and limited control loops for actuators. Services are targeted at facility managers, designers, and researchers, enabling them to inspect current conditions, run performance simulations, obtain optimization recommendations, and visualize outcomes through an immersive Unreal Engine interface.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on the Eclipse Hono IoT gateway for secure, scalable ingestion of MQTT/HTTP sensor data, Eclipse Ditto as the core digital‑twin model and service platform, and Unreal Engine 5 for high‑fidelity, interactive 3D visualization. The static building description is grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM), which is synchronized with live data via the Hono‑Ditto integration layer. Additional enablers include standard data exchange protocols (MQTT, HTTP) and a cloud‑based persistence layer for historical data.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes the physical building with its digital replica. Updates are propagated as soon as sensor readings or control actions occur, implying a near‑zero latency loop suitable for device‑level monitoring and control. Temporal granularity is at the level of individual building devices (e.g., HVAC units, lighting fixtures). No explicit numerical frequency is specified.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DT Framework is realised as a central hub that hosts a single digital twin instance for each building. The architecture is essentially centralized; multiple stakeholder roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) interact with the same hub. Coordination among twins is performed through the hub’s bidirectional data exchange; no distributed or hierarchical twin federation is described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture with standardized extension points. Core components (BIM enrichment, data acquisition, visualization, runtime configuration) are plugged into the central DT Framework, which orchestrates their interaction. Resource management is handled at runtime through configuration changes without redeployment. System‑wide coordination is achieved via the hub that routes data between physical devices, BIM models, and custom analytics services.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability is addressed through non‑functional requirements that demand compatibility with heterogeneous systems. The framework relies on standardized extension points and open interfaces to integrate external services, analytics, and enterprise systems. Specific data exchange protocols are not enumerated, but the design emphasizes modular integration and bidirectional communication with external components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches BIM geometric models with semantic definitions of sensors, actuators, and controllers, creating a unified semantic foundation. Building Control Engineers model these devices directly within the BIM environment; the models are then processed using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts. Physical sensors and actuators serve as the primary data sources, and their relationships to the underlying geometric BIM model are maintained through the unified semantic layer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuating elements are introduced in the BIM environment as actuator entities linked to controller logic defined by the Building Control Engineer. The generated control logic is executed by the Digital Twin and exposed through remote service endpoints that allow the Facility Manager to command the physical infrastructure via the DT. Specific actuator types, ranges, safety constraints or addressing schemes are not detailed in the document, but each modeled actuator can be addressed through the DT’s remote interface for operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled as first‑class entities within the enriched BIM model, with properties describing measurement type, location and expected data format. Real‑time data exchange mechanisms keep live sensor telemetry synchronized with the Digital Twin, ensuring the DT reflects current measurements. The document does not provide concrete sensor types, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or accuracy specifications.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework evaluates the correspondence between the DT and its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, which quantifies how well the generated DT reflects the real building. Validation is performed by comparing runtime telemetry (sensor streams, actuator states) against the modeled expectations, enabling detection of deviations. Uncertainty sources such as modeling gaps in BIM are acknowledged, and the ODTE score serves as a quality‑assurance indicator guiding further refinement of models and data pipelines.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a full DT lifecycle that starts with device modeling directly in the BIM environment (defining sensors, actuators, controllers), proceeds to model‑driven engineering (MDE) that extracts a structured representation and automatically generates deployable DT artifacts, followed by runtime operation where continuous real‑time data exchange keeps the physical building and its digital counterpart synchronized, and interactive 3‑D visualization enables monitoring and control. The lifecycle is extensible through standardized extension points that allow integration of custom services, supporting ongoing evolution and governance of the twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT engineering follows a model‑driven process: Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with device definitions and control logic; a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline automatically transforms these enriched models into executable DT components; DT Engineers develop and plug‑in custom services via the framework’s modular extension points. The process emphasizes automated generation, minimal manual intervention, and modular evolution, though the document does not detail explicit quality‑assurance, testing, or version‑control practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides operational decision support through continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps physical sensor/actuator states synchronized with the digital model, and an immersive interactive visualization interface that allows Facility Managers to monitor building performance and issue control actions directly from the DT. No specific analytics, predictive insights, or confidence metrics are described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution is built to be compatible with existing AECO‑specific standards such as BIM, ensuring that device models and control logic are anchored in industry‑accepted building information models. For interoperability and real‑time data exchange, the framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, leveraging their IoT communication and digital twin protocols to connect physical devices with the twin. The architecture also follows a modular, standardized extension‑point model to facilitate compliance with broader interoperability frameworks.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately identifies the 'Living Lab' as the physical twin and research platform within the AECO domain, which is supported by the text's reference to 'a DT of a Living Lab' used as a case study. It correctly describes the physical twin's composition, including building infrastructure (envelope, HVAC, lighting, MEP) and the presence of sensors and actuators, aligning with the text's discussion of 'sensor configurations, control logic, and live operational data' embedded in BIM. The mention of BIM capturing static information is well-supported by the text's emphasis on 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and BIM as a 'semantic backbone'. While the specific sensor types (temperature, humidity, occupancy, energy) are inferred from the general context of building operations and the projects mentioned (e.g., TwinLight's focus on lighting), the description is relevant and specific to the system described.

The extracted services are well-supported by the source text. Real-time monitoring is explicitly mentioned in the abstract: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization'. Interactive 3D visualization is supported by the text's reference to 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'. Simulation of scenarios and optimization are consistent with the text's description of DTs enabling 'simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'life cycle-encompassing optimization'. Predictive analytics are mentioned in the introduction as a capability of DTs: 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics'. The target users (facility managers, designers, researchers) align with the roles defined in Table I (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer). The extraction is detailed and strongly grounded in the text.

The extraction correctly identifies the key tools and their functionalities. Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto are explicitly mentioned as the components for real-time data exchange: 'integrating with Eclipse Hono and Ditto, our proposal supports real-time data exchange'. Unreal Engine 5 is correctly cited for visualization: 'Unreal Engine 5 provides a rich visualization environment'. BIM is correctly described as the foundation for static building information, consistent with the text's repeated emphasis on 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and BIM as a 'semantic backbone'. The mention of MQTT/HTTP as standard protocols is inferred from the general context of IoT and real-time data exchange, but the core tools and their roles are highly specific and well-supported.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the framework's use of real-time data exchange and continuous synchronization, and accurately notes the absence of a specific numerical frequency. However, it adds specific examples of devices ('HVAC units, 'lighting fixtures') that are not present in the source text (which only mentions 'lighting systems' and general 'infrastructure'), and infers 'near-zero latency' from 'real-time', which constitutes minor speculation. Supported by quotes: 'real-time data exchange', 'continuous synchronization', 'live synchronization'.

The description accurately captures the centralized 'hub' architecture and the interaction of multiple stakeholder roles with this hub. The claim that the hub hosts a 'single digital twin instance for each building' is a reasonable inference given the text's focus on 'a DT of a Living Lab' and 'virtual representations of physical assets', though the text does not explicitly constrain the multiplicities to one per building. The description of the architecture as centralized is well-supported. Supported by quotes: 'DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'Building Control Engineers... Facility Managers... DT Engineers', 'real-time data exchange'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the system's orchestration, including its modular nature, extension points, runtime configuration capabilities, and the role of the central hub. It accurately synthesizes the components mentioned or implied in the text (BIM, visualization, custom services). Supported by quotes: 'modular architecture with defined extension points', 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability by enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment', 'central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange'.

The extraction accurately reflects the non-functional requirements regarding interoperability and heterogeneous systems. It correctly identifies the reliance on standardized extension points and the lack of enumerated protocols (only tools like Hono/Ditto are mentioned). The emphasis on modular integration is consistent with the text. Supported by quotes: 'non-functional requirements (NFX) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems', 'standardized extension points', 'bidirectional communication'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the framework's use of BIM enriched with semantic definitions of devices, the role of Building Control Engineers, and the MDE-based generation of DT artifacts. It is well-supported by the source text, which details these exact processes. Quotes: 'This enables building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'.

The extraction correctly describes the modeling of actuators in BIM by Building Control Engineers and the remote operation by Facility Managers. It also accurately notes that specific ranges or safety constraints are not detailed in the provided text.

The extraction correctly identifies sensors as modeled entities within BIM and the use of real-time data exchange for synchronization. It accurately notes the absence of specific technical details like sampling rates or accuracy in the source documents.

The extraction correctly identifies the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric as the evaluation method and accurately describes its purpose as measuring how well the DT reflects the physical counterpart, supported by quotes like 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'. It also correctly acknowledges uncertainty sources such as modeling gaps (C1). While the specific mechanism of comparing telemetry against 'modeled expectations' is a reasonable inference of 'entanglement' and 'reflects its physical counterpart', the overall description is technically detailed and well-grounded.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

It discusses data fragmentation and integration challenges but does not mention specific data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with regulations, or consent management. Therefore, the assessment that this characteristic is not in the document is accurate.

The provided document details a framework for Digital Twin deployment, including real-time data exchange via Eclipse Hono and Ditto. However, it does not describe specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms. Consequently, stating that this characteristic is not in the document is correct.
